items.title: Gocloud — designing a unified API library for AWS, GCP, and Azure
items.summary: What it actually takes to build a unified cloud API library — and why "write once, run anywhere" still doesn't quite work, even for the patterns where it almost does.

items.title: Google Summer of Code mentor 2019-2026 — what I learned guiding 10+ students through open-source projects
items.summary: Seven cycles. Ten-plus students. Most shipped, a few didn't, all of them taught me something about engineering culture. Notes on what works for mentors and what works for students.

items.title: Speaking at Google Cloud Next 2022 — the monolith-to-microservices talk
items.summary: 30 minutes on stage. The talk title looked tactical; the talk underneath was about why most microservices migrations fail and how to set up the one that doesn't.

items.title: Brownlow — zero-trust voting on Cloud Run during live AFL broadcasts
items.summary: 100K+ votes, 10K+ concurrent users during a live AFL Brownlow Medal broadcast. The architecture: Go on Cloud Run, GraphQL + gRPC behind a CDN, vote integrity through Cloud KMS + Security Command Center. Notes on what makes a live-broadcast load shape unusual.

items.title: P2P lending — KYC/AML, three credit bureaus, and the maker-checker RBAC governance that kept fraud rates low
items.summary: Borrower onboarding is the most fraud-prone moment in a P2P platform. The shape that worked: deterministic KYC, parallel bureau pulls with fallback, real-time fraud signals, and a maker-checker approval for every disbursement.

items.title: Double-entry ledger invariants — building a P2P lending platform that never loses money
items.summary: 5K+ loans per month. Three credit bureaus. Multiple payment gateways. The thing that has to be right is the ledger. Notes on what invariants the database enforces vs what the application enforces.

items.title: Error-code orchestration — replacing a giant switch statement with a typed enum across partner adapters
items.summary: Status-code-based dispatch made every worker grow a longer and longer switch. Normalising every partner-specific error into an enumerated set let the orchestration logic stop changing as new partners landed.

items.title: Idempotency at three layers — the pattern that kept the Globe transaction engine honest
items.summary: A single layer of idempotency will eventually fail. Three independent layers gives you a margin. Here is the pattern that worked across ingest, worker, and emit boundaries.

items.title: Globe — running a 30K+ TPS transaction platform on Kubernetes
items.summary: The transaction engine had to absorb 30K+ TPS across partner integrations, never lose a transaction, and survive partial failures. The architecture: Go, Kafka, Pub/Sub, Redis, K8s, with idempotency at every layer.

items.title: Picnic — 80%+ test coverage and Prometheus observability turned days-to-detect into minutes-to-detect
items.summary: Test coverage and observability are the boring infrastructure that makes the interesting changes safe. Notes on how the Picnic team built both, and the on-call experience they enabled.

items.title: Picnic — cutting API latency 47% by consolidating microservices behind protobuf contracts
items.summary: The Picnic social platform served 1M+ users across a graph of Go microservices behind a GraphQL gateway. The latency win came from a counter-intuitive move: fewer services, tighter contracts.

items.title: azure-service-operator — collaborating with Ericsson, AT&T, and Microsoft on a Kubernetes-native Azure controller
items.summary: The azure-service-operator project lets you declare Azure resources as Kubernetes objects. Notes from the multi-vendor collaboration shape: how decisions got made, what slowed us down, what shipped despite it.

items.title: airshipit + OpenTelemetry — 30% reduction in manual ops in a multi-vendor OSS project
items.summary: Notes from integrating OpenTelemetry into airshipit, an open-source bare-metal Kubernetes lifecycle project with contributions from Ericsson, AT&T, Microsoft, and others. The hard part wasn't OTel; it was making distributed traces useful across foreign code.

items.title: SOC 2 controls as Terraform modules — turning the audit from a project into a daily build
items.summary: If you encode each SOC 2 control as a Terraform module, the audit becomes a check against module usage rather than a per-resource review. Notes from Bloom and adjacent projects.

items.title: Bloom — Terraform for regulated bank cloud provisioning, SOC 2 and ISO 27001 from day one
items.summary: Notes from contributing to Bloom — SC Ventures / Standard Chartered's policy-driven secure cloud provisioning platform. Push-to-deploy self-service for bank engineering teams, with the audit controls baked in.

items.title: CDC for minimal-downtime Spanner migration — Datastream + Pub/Sub + Dataflow
items.summary: A bulk migration takes hours; the application can't be offline that long. CDC keeps the source and destination in sync while the bulk runs, and a quick cutover swaps traffic. The handoff between bulk and CDC is where most migrations go wrong.

items.title: Spanner interleaved tables — when and when not
items.summary: Interleaving a child table into its parent co-locates the rows for fast joins. It also tightens coupling in ways that bite you on the next schema migration. A practitioner's decision matrix.

items.title: Primary-key design for Cloud Spanner — preventing write hotspots, 40-60% performance gains
items.summary: Spanner partitions by primary-key range. A monotonically-increasing PK like a timestamp or UUID-v1 funnels all writes to one server. The fix changes everything from your sequence strategy to your tenant model.

items.title: The Spanner Migration Tool — a contributor's reading map
items.summary: Notes from contributing to Google's open-source Spanner Migration Tool (HarbourBridge). Where to start reading the codebase, where the load-bearing logic lives, and the parts that look simple but aren't.

items.title: BigQuery storage tiering — physical bytes, logical bytes, and the savings hiding in your old partitions
items.summary: Storage was the second-biggest line item on the Tata BigQuery bill. Long-term storage, physical-vs-logical billing, and column-level retention together took a 6-figure monthly line down to a 5-figure one.

items.title: BigQuery slot reservation transitions — when to commit, when to stay on-demand
items.summary: Capacity-based slot reservation is the biggest single FinOps lever for predictable batch workloads, but the transition is harder than the math. Notes from sizing reservations across enterprise GCP customers.

items.title: Optimus — a Gemini-powered BigQuery anti-pattern detector that paid for itself in a week
items.summary: We built a small Go + Python service that parses a project's INFORMATION_SCHEMA, asks Gemini to classify each top-spending query against a catalog of anti-patterns, and recommends a rewrite. It is not a magic box; it is a pipeline that cuts the human review time per query from 20 minutes to 90 seconds.

items.title: The 57% number — how we cut the Tata Group BigQuery bill in half
items.summary: ₹100 Cr / ~$12M in proven savings across a year-plus engagement. The four levers that did the heavy lifting, the lever I expected to win that didn't, and the post-engagement playbook that became a Searce managed service.

items.title: The MERGE pattern that cost ten times more than INSERT-then-UPDATE — a ₹100 Cr lesson
items.summary: What looked like an idiomatic BigQuery MERGE was scanning the full target table on every batch. The fix was syntactic, not architectural — and it was the single biggest contributor to a 57% data-warehouse cost reduction across the Tata Group engagement.

items.title: The Right-to-Explanation Handler: GDPR Article 22 as a Go HTTP Endpoint
items.summary: How a 200-line Go handler turns an audit log and an eval store into a regulator-friendly answer to "why did the AI decide that?" — without leaking a single byte of PHI.

items.title: Mapping a multi-agent platform to the GCP PCSE blueprint
items.summary: Every Professional Cloud Security Engineer exam bullet, mapped to a file path in an RBI FREE-AI aligned Go platform. Where the implementation matches, where the analog substitutes, and where the honest gaps are.

items.title: Time-bound privileged access — building the PCSE §1.4 PAM analog in Go
items.summary: Request → N-eyes approve → window-of-time → automatic expiry, with every transition written to a hash-chained audit log. The package that closes Gap #1 from the PCSE map.

items.title: Defence in depth for agentic AI — the eleven-layer envelope
items.summary: The mental model that says no two adjacent layers share a single point of failure for the same class of attack. From TLS to OTel, the eleven layers a customer request crosses before an answer comes back.

items.title: Agentic security in production — the operations playbook
items.summary: Twelve months of running multi-agent AI in a regulated context. SLIs that matter, the incident runbook, drift detection, continuous adversarial testing, secret rotation, compliance posture as code.

items.title: Consolidated security deep-dive — RBAC, RLS, token exchange, encryption, audit
items.summary: The long-form security narrative for a multi-agent financial assistant — authentication, authorisation, tenant isolation, dual-identity audit, envelope encryption, hash-chained logs, governance, red team, BCP.

items.title: AI governance — from credential to codebase
items.summary: Board policy as a YAML file the risk team owns. Annexure VI as a database query. Every governance recommendation rendered as a file path in a Go repository.

items.title: Why Go for production agentic AI
items.summary: Stdlib over libraries, single binary over framework, fail-closed defaults over forgiveness. The boring-on-purpose case for choosing Go to ship a multi-agent system into a regulated environment.

items.title: Deterministic KYC, the LLM just talks
items.summary: PAN check-digit validation, Aadhaar offline KYC, DigiLocker, PEP/sanctions — all in Go code, not in a prompt. The LLM's job is to translate the verdict into something a human can read.

items.title: Policy as code, without the risk team having to ship code
items.summary: A tiny CEL-style DSL plus a board-approved YAML file. The risk team adds a governance rule by editing a config file; engineering ships the rule by restarting the service.

items.title: Annexure VI as a query
items.summary: The RBI FREE-AI incident reporting form, expressed as a Go struct and a Postgres table. Every entry is an auto-generated artefact from the runtime — not a form an operator fills in retrospectively.

items.title: NPCI rail routing with human-in-the-loop
items.summary: UPI, IMPS, NEFT, RTGS — which rail to use depends on amount, urgency, window, success-rate history. A deterministic chooser with a HITL gate above ₹2 lakh.

items.title: Sovereign AI is a policy, not a slide
items.summary: Classification → provider allowlist. A pii-classified message can only reach a provider whose region is in the allowlist for pii. Sovereignty as a runtime gate, not a checkbox.

items.title: BCP for AI — forced-failure drills
items.summary: Fallback agents plus a CI step that replaces the primary agent with one that always errors. If the fallback doesn't produce a usable answer, the PR can't merge.

items.title: RBI FREE-AI implementation notes — 26 recommendations to file paths
items.summary: Every one of the 26 RBI FREE-AI recommendations, mapped to a specific file in a working multi-agent platform. What's ✅ done, what's 🟡 partial, what's ⚪ honest gap.

items.title: Production agentic AI on Kubernetes — Ch 9 patterns vs reality
items.summary: Field notes from running multi-agent AI on K8s. The patterns the book recommends, the ones that survived contact with production, and the ones that broke in interesting ways.

items.title: Agentic architecture on MARA — the seven load-bearing pieces
items.summary: Microsoft's Multi-Agent Reference Architecture in Go. Protocol, registry, bus, governance, orchestration, observability, evaluation — and how the seven hold each other up.
